original_abstract: Precise control over dimensions and spatially defined functionality remains a central challenge in nanoparticle engineering. Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has recently gained prominence as a robust strategy for producing one-dimensional and two-dimensional core–shell micellar architectures via the seeded growth of polymeric and molecular amphiphiles. However, a major bottleneck remains: epitaxial growth is typically restricted to a single core chemistry. This limitation hinders the fabrication of complex nanoparticles featuring segmented cores with diverse compositions—architectures that are critical for achieving selective chemical transformations or specialized responses to external stimuli. In this study, we demonstrate a seeded growth approach utilizing various crystallizable polylactone homopolymer and block copolymer blend combinations to produce 2D platelet micelles with compositionally distinct, segmented cores. To highlight the practical utility of this structural control, we show that the internal core chemistry can be exploited to achieve spatially selective hydrolytic degradation. These findings offer a blueprint for designing complex, stimuli-responsive particles tailored for programmed-release and advanced cargo-delivery applications.
humanized_abstract: Controlling the size and functional properties of individual nanoparticles remains an important issue. Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has been employed to obtain a wide range of one dimensional and two dimensional core–shell micellar architectures through the seeded growth of a variety of amphiphilic polymeric and molecular chains. However, most of these examples have demonstrated the ability of epitaxial growth of a single core species. Multi-species crystalline cores are highly required in order to achieve sequential or spatially selective chemical reactions along with functional response to diverse external stimuli, all of which cannot be achieved by the current single-core structure. Here, we for the first time, through the seeded growth of different homopolymer and copolymer blends of crystallizable polylactone-based materials, obtained two dimensional 2D platelet micelles with a range of different segmented multi-species cores. Moreover, we have shown an example of core composition triggered functional properties by exemplifying that the different core components can be utilized to carry out spatially selective hydrolytic degradation of the corresponding polymers. Therefore, this work paves the way to design and fabrication of functional particles for variety of programmed-release as well as cargo-based delivery applications.
